feedback.text: I commend the initiative for the Digital Fairness Act and the commitment to enhancing consumer protections in the digital age. I am writing to urge you to include a critical issue in this Act: the practice of publishers permanently disabling video games that consumers have purchased. This is a matter of immense importance, as demonstrated by the European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames," which has garnered the support of over 1.4 million EU citizens. The core of the problem is a deceptive sales model. We buy video games under the clear impression of a one-time purchase, yet what we receive is effectively a temporary license that can be revoked at any time, rendering the product we paid for completely worthless. This is fundamentally unfair and undermines consumer trust. Beyond protecting our investments, this issue is also about preserving our shared cultural heritage. Video games are a significant artistic and cultural medium of our time, just as vital as books, music, and film. To allow them to be permanently erased is to allow a vital part of our modern culture to vanish forever. Future generations of creators, researchers, and citizens will be unable to experience, study, or learn from these interactive works. Therefore, I strongly request that the Digital Fairness Act introduces clear regulations to address this: A Right to Lasting Playability: Publishers must be required to provide a patch or other means to ensure games remain playable on consumer hardware or through community-run servers after official online support is discontinued. The core product that was sold must continue to function. Transparency in Licensing: If a game is designed to be temporary, it must be explicitly sold as a time-limited subscription, not a "purchase." Consumers deserve to know exactly what they are paying for and for how long it will last. A Framework for Cultural Archiving: Legal provisions should be made to allow designated public institutions, such as national libraries and museums, to archive and preserve video games as cultural artifacts, bypassing DRM for this specific, non-commercial purpose. By incorporating these protections, the Digital Fairness Act will not only safeguard consumer rights but also affirm Europe's commitment to preserving its rich and evolving cultural landscape. Thank you for your time and for considering this crucial addition to the Act.
